front: Figure: served as Lord Protector of England, Scotland, and Ireland
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: Puritan general who took control of the New Model Army from Thomas Fairfax
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: nicknamed "Old Ironsides," a name derived from his cavalry unit's nickname
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: led the Roundhead faction during the English Civil War
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Battle: Cromwell helped command the New Model Army to victory here (1645) as second-in-command under Thomas Fairfax, with whom he later feuded
work: English Civil War: Rise to Power

front: Figure: commanded the "Ironsides" cavalry unit; was the father-in-law of Henry Ireton
work: English Civil War: Rise to Power

front: Battle: Cromwell won the last battle of the (First) English Civil War here in 1648
work: English Civil War: Rise to Power

front: Event: Cromwell conducted this siege during the Second English Civil War, suppressing a Royalist revolt in Wales
work: English Civil War: Rise to Power

front: Event: Cromwell was among the regicides who signed this document (traditionally counted its third signatory), sending the king to the block
work: English Civil War: Rise to Power

front: Battle: Cromwell defeated Charles II and an invading Scottish army here in 1651, after which Charles hid in an oak tree at Boscobel House
work: English Civil War: Rise to Power

front: Event: at this siege, defending commander Arthur Aston was legendarily beaten to death with his own wooden leg, believed to conceal gold coins
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Event: at this siege, New Model Army troops shot or drowned over 1,000 civilians in the River Slaney after Cromwell rejected governor David Sinnot's surrender negotiations
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Battle: victory here let Cromwell's troops, including those under Henry Ireton, land in Ireland to begin the conquest
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Event: Cromwell's conquest of Ireland brought this conflict to an end
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Law: these laws, passed after Cromwell's conquest, forced Irish Catholics to convert to Protestantism
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Law: this Rump Parliament act confiscated land and forced surviving Irish Catholic landowners to relocate to Connacht ("to Hell or Connacht")
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Law: this earlier act rewarded private citizens who had invested in the government with confiscated Irish land
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Figure: nickname for poor colonists deported to Barbados during Cromwell's conquest of Ireland
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Event: Cromwell's land settlement heavily favored Protestant settlers in this province, many of whom had been massacred in the 1641 rebellion
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Institution: Cromwell ordered this early national land survey of Ireland
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Battle: a setback during Cromwell's conquest of Ireland occurred at this city
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Institution: Cromwell's conquest crushed this confederation of Irish Catholics
work: Conquest of Ireland

front: Document: this 1653 document, England's only written constitution, was based on the "Heads of Proposals" and restricted the franchise to those owning over 200 pounds of property
work: Lord Protector: Government of the Protectorate

front: Event: Cromwell dismissed this Parliament by pointing at the ceremonial mace and declaring "take away that fool's bauble"
work: Lord Protector: Government of the Protectorate

front: Institution: Cromwell replaced the dissolved Rump Parliament with this nominated assembly in 1653
work: Lord Protector: Government of the Protectorate

front: Institution: under this 1655-57 system, Cromwell divided England into twelve military districts policed by militias funded by a "decimation tax"
work: Lord Protector: Government of the Protectorate

front: Document: this 1657 settlement ended the Rule of the Major-Generals, overruled the Instrument of Government, and created the "Other House"
work: Lord Protector: Government of the Protectorate

front: Event: Cromwell convened this conference to debate, and ultimately favor, readmitting Jews to England
work: Lord Protector: Government of the Protectorate

front: Event: Cromwell chaired this 1647 disputation against the Levellers, opposed there by Colonel Thomas Rainsborough and supported by his son-in-law Henry Ireton
work: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots

front: Event: Cromwell suppressed this mutiny after troops refused to pledge loyalty to Thomas Fairfax
work: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots

front: Event: after this mutiny, prompted by a regiment's refusal to serve in Ireland over the violated Solemn Engagement, Cromwell executed three Leveller soldiers at Burford
work: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots

front: Event: Cromwell quelled this mutiny, which led to the execution of Robert Lockyer
work: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots

front: Figure: these millenarian radicals criticized Cromwell as "the dissemblingest perjured villain in the world" after he became Lord Protector in 1653
work: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots

front: Institution: Cromwell smashed this Royalist conspiracy using intelligence gathered by spymaster John Thurloe
work: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots

front: Event: this 1657 pamphlet advocated Cromwell's assassination, written after Miles Sindercombe's failed plot to burn Whitehall Palace
work: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots

front: Event: Cromwell commissioned William Penn and Robert Venables to lead this campaign against the Spanish West Indies, which took Jamaica but failed to capture Hispaniola
work: Foreign & Colonial Policy

front: Figure: this admiral invaded the pro-Royalist Isles of Scilly and destroyed King Charles's Castle, leading to the building of Cromwell's Castle
work: Foreign & Colonial Policy

front: Institution: an alliance with France gained England this port under Cromwell
work: Foreign & Colonial Policy

front: Figure: this man left his post as Governor of Bermuda to lead an expedition to Preacher's Cave in support of Cromwell's government
work: Foreign & Colonial Policy

front: Figure: this son succeeded Cromwell as Lord Protector, but his incompetence led Parliament to restore Charles II
work: Death, Succession & Posthumous Execution

front: Event: after the Restoration, Cromwell's corpse was disinterred and posthumously "executed"; his head eventually reached this Cambridge college
work: Death, Succession & Posthumous Execution

front: Work: this poem calls Cromwell the "restless" man who "could not cease / In the inglorious arts of peace"
back: "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland" (Andrew Marvell)
work: Cultural Depictions: Andrew Marvell's Cromwell Odes

front: Work: this poem compares Cromwell to "three-fork'd lightning" blasting through the laurels on "Caesar's head"
back: "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland" (Andrew Marvell)
work: Cultural Depictions: Andrew Marvell's Cromwell Odes

front: Work: this poem says "The Pict no shelter now shall find" within Cromwell's "parti-colour'd mind," comparing him to Caesar and Hannibal
back: "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland" (Andrew Marvell)
work: Cultural Depictions: Andrew Marvell's Cromwell Odes

front: Work: this poem contrasts Cromwell's restlessness with another figure who lived "reserved and austere" as if his "highest plot" were "to plant the bergamot"
back: "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland" (Andrew Marvell)
work: Cultural Depictions: Andrew Marvell's Cromwell Odes

front: Work: this poem's penultimate stanza calls Cromwell "the War's and Fortune's son" and urges him to "march indefatigably on"
back: "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland" (Andrew Marvell)
work: Cultural Depictions: Andrew Marvell's Cromwell Odes

front: Work: this poem says Cromwell "contracted" the "force of scattered time" and was "chosen out of better Earth" than other kings
work: Cultural Depictions: Andrew Marvell's Cromwell Odes

front: Work: Cromwell is the subject of this Milton sonnet, "To the Lord General Cromwell, May 1652"
work: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments

front: Figure: wrote an early "Panegyrick" praising Cromwell
work: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments

front: Work: John Dryden praised Cromwell in this poem before switching allegiance to Charles II in "Astraea Redux"
back: "Heroic Stanzas on the Death of Oliver Cromwell" (John Dryden)
work: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments

front: Work: this Thomas Gray poem imagines a village dead who might have been "some Cromwell guiltless of his country's blood"
back: "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard" (Thomas Gray)
work: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments

front: Figure: this historian cited Cromwell as an example of a hero in On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History, part of his "great man theory"
work: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments

front: Work: this T. S. Eliot essay cites Marvell's treatment of Cromwell as an example of the poet's "proper degree of seriousness"
work: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments

front: Work: Cromwell is the title figure of this Victor Hugo verse play, whose preface became a manifesto for Romantic French drama
work: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments
